title: Lavender compress for insect bites
subtitle: A cooling compress to ease the itch and swelling of a bite
excerpt: A cooled lavender infusion applied as a compress to insect bites and mild stings. The lavender reduces localised inflammation and soothes the itch. One of the simplest topical applications of lavender in the domestic tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry, anti-inflammatory and antiseptic topical properties. Traditional European domestic use of lavender for bites and skin irritation, documented in household herbals from the 18th and 19th centuries.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh for each session. A cooled infusion can be stored in the fridge for up to 12 hours if needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb applied to the skin surface that reduces localised redness, swelling, and heat, in this context, the inflammatory response to an insect bite.

content.text: A cooled lavender infusion applied as a compress to a fresh insect bite or mild sting. Lavender's

and antiseptic properties make it one of the most straightforward topical applications in the domestic herbal tradition, Maud Grieve notes lavender's antiseptic and soothing action on the skin, and its use for bites is recorded in household herbals going back centuries. The compress is cooling (which relieves the itch directly), anti-inflammatory (which reduces the wheal), and mildly antiseptic (which reduces the risk of secondary infection from scratching). The preparation is a covered lavender infusion, cooled to room temperature or refrigerator-cold, and applied on a clean cloth pad.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Skin sensitivity: lavender at infusion strength is well tolerated on most skin. For sensitive or eczema-prone skin, test on a small patch of unbroken skin first.

Allergic reactions to insect stings: anaphylaxis, a severe allergic reaction to bee or wasp stings, is a medical emergency. Symptoms include difficulty breathing, throat swelling, rapid heart rate, dizziness, and widespread hives within minutes of the sting. If any of these occur, call 999 immediately. Carry an adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) if you have a known sting allergy, and do not treat anaphylaxis with herbal preparations.

See your doctor for: large local reactions (swelling of a whole limb), bites that show signs of infection after 24, 48 hours (spreading redness, heat, pus), bites from unfamiliar insects abroad, or any bite in a child that causes disproportionate distress or systemic symptoms.

items.prepNote: just boiled, then cooled to room temperature or refrigerator-cold before use

content.text: You will also need a clean cotton pad or a square of soft clean cloth, and a small clean bowl to hold the infusion while you dip the pad.

Place {{dried-lavender-flowers}} of dried lavender flowers in the teapot. Pour {{water}} of just-boiled water over, lid immediately, and steep for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a clean bowl. Allow to cool completely, a cold or cool compress is more effective on an inflamed bite than a warm one. Soak a clean cotton pad in the cool infusion, squeeze out the excess, and hold gently against the bite for five minutes. Repeat two to three times. For intense itching, a refrigerator-cold compress gives immediate relief.

Apply the compress two to three times during the first 24 hours after the bite. By 24 to 48 hours, most insect bite reactions are past their peak. If itching is severe, the compress can be applied hourly, there are no safety concerns with frequent topical lavender use on intact skin.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use for suspected anaphylaxis, this is an emergency requiring adrenaline and 999. Do not apply to open or broken skin. See your doctor for bites that show signs of infection (spreading redness, increasing pain, pus), a whole-limb swelling after a sting, or any bite that causes systemic symptoms beyond local itching and swelling.
